sections.heading: What is a Percent Error Calculator?
sections.content: A Percent Error Calculator is an online tool that calculates the difference between your measured (observed) value and the true (accepted) value as a percentage. It helps determine the accuracy of experiments, predictions, or calculations. The result is always positive because it uses the absolute difference.

sections.heading: Formula used:
sections.content:
$$Percentage Error = \frac{|Observed Value - True Value|}{|True Value|} \times 100\%$$ 
 Note: The result is always positive since it uses the absolute difference.

sections.heading: How to Use the Percent Error Calculator
sections.content: This Percentage Error Calculator is simple and beginner-friendly. Just enter your values and get instant results.

subSections.heading: Steps to Use:
subSections.content: Enter the Observed Value (your measured or estimated result). Enter the True Value (accepted or reference value). Click Calculate to get the percent error instantly. Analyze the Result — a smaller percentage indicates higher accuracy.

sections.heading: Key Features of a Percent Error Calculator
sections.content: The tool provides accurate, quick, and user-friendly percent error results.

subSections.heading: User-Friendly Interface
subSections.content: No technical or mathematical expertise required.

subSections.heading: Accurate Formula
subSections.content: Uses the standard percent error formula for reliable results.

subSections.heading: Supports Various Inputs
subSections.content: Works with whole numbers, decimals, or percentages.

subSections.heading: Free and Safe
subSections.content: No installation required and no personal data collected.

sections.heading: Use Cases of a Percentage Error Calculator
sections.content: Percent error is widely used across different fields to measure accuracy.

subSections.heading: Academics
subSections.content: Used in physics, chemistry, and math labs to compare experimental results.

subSections.heading: Engineering
subSections.content: Ensures measurements and designs match required standards.

subSections.heading: Finance
subSections.content: Compares predicted vs actual results in forecasts or budgets.

subSections.heading: Quality Control
subSections.content: Monitors the consistency and accuracy of production results.

subSections.heading: Everyday Tasks
subSections.content: Useful for estimates, recipes, DIY measurements, and more.

subSections.heading: Example 2 – Predicted Sales:
subSections.content:
Predicted Sales: 200 units 
 Actual Sales: 220 units 
 $$Percentage Error = \frac{|200 - 220|}{220} \times 100 \approx 9.09\%$$

subSections.heading: Example 3 – Construction Measurement:
subSections.content:
Measured Length: 9.7m 
 Actual Length: 10m 
 $$Percentage Error = \frac{|9.7 - 10|}{10} \times 100 = 3\%$$ 
 These examples demonstrate the practical utility of a Percent Error Calculator.

faq.q: What is the difference between Percent Error and Percentage Error?
faq.a: There is no difference — both terms describe the same concept of measurement accuracy.

faq.q: Why is percent error always positive?
faq.a: Because it uses the absolute value of the difference between observed and true values.

faq.q: Can I calculate percent error manually?
Is this calculator suitable for decimals?
faq.a: Yes, it works with both decimals and whole numbers.

faq.q: Can I calculate percent error for multiple measurements?
faq.a: Yes, but calculate each measurement separately to get individual percent errors.

faq.q: Is it safe to use online Percent Error Calculators?
faq.a: Yes, reputable tools are free, secure, and do not collect personal data.

faq.q: Why should I use a calculator instead of manual calculation?
faq.a: It saves time, reduces mistakes, and provides instant and accurate results.

faq.q: Can I use this in financial analysis?
faq.a: Yes, percent error is useful for comparing predicted and actual outcomes.

faq.q: What does a low percent error indicate?
faq.a: It means your observed value is very close to the true value, showing high accuracy.

faq.q: Can percent error be greater than 100%?
faq.a: Yes, if the observed value is significantly higher or lower than the true value.
